articles.title: Urban water crises driven by elites' unsustainable consumption
data_sources: SA Dept. of Water and Sanitation Hydrological Data Service
City of Cape Town 2020 Census report
Primary fieldwork (65 interviews + 5 focus groups, May 2019 - Mar 2020)
articles.core_conclusion: In Cape Town, elite and upper-middle-income households (13.7% of the population) consume over 51% of the city's water, while informal and lower-income residents (61.5%) share only 27.3%. A system-dynamic model shows that unsustainable elite overconsumption is at least as damaging to the long-term urban water balance as climate change or population growth, and equitable water redistribution would have substantially mitigated the Day Zero crisis.

articles.title: Global mapping of urban nature-based solutions for climate change adaptation
methods: systematic mapping (Collaboration for Environmental Evidence protocol)
data_sources: World Bank NbS for Disaster Risk Reduction portfolio
Urban Climate Change Research Network Case Study Docking Station
OpenStreetMap (city base maps, CC BY SA)
articles.core_conclusion: A systematic global mapping of 216 urban NbS interventions across 130 cities in 55 countries finds that current urban NbS practice is largely incremental and reformistic — not transformative. Practice is heavily biased toward the Global North (63% in Europe) and narrowly addresses climate–biodiversity–society (CBS) challenges, particularly underrepresenting social justice dimensions (recognition and distributional equity), private-sector engagement, and the climate hazards (drought, coastal) most severe in the Global South. Fulfilling NbS transformative potential requires expansion in geography, governance models, and social equity frameworks.

articles.title: A symbolic Neanderthal accumulation of large herbivore crania
methods: skeletal element quantification (NISP, MNE, MAU, %MAU, MNI)
3D spatial recording and GIS analysis (AutoCAD 2021, QGIS v.3.22)
AMS radiocarbon dating (Oxford; OxCal v.4.4, IntCal20)
data_sources: Cueva Des-Cubierta faunal bone assemblage (physical specimens)
AMS radiocarbon dates (Oxford Radiocarbon Accelerator Unit)
U/Th speleothem dates (CENIEH; Jaume Almera CSIC)
Experimental butchering reference dataset (3 cow heads)
micro-CT scans of Neanderthal mandible and deciduous teeth (CENIEH)
articles.core_conclusion: Neanderthals at Cueva Des-Cubierta (central Spain) repeatedly brought the crania of large herbivores — bison, aurochs, red deer, and rhinoceros — into the cave over a sustained period spanning the full depth of Level 3, associating them with small hearths and percussion tools. Taphonomic, taxonomic, spatial, and experimental evidence together rule out subsistence, carnivore denning, or natural accumulation as explanations. The recurrent, cross-generational nature of the behaviour strongly implies a symbolic or ritual function, extending the documented range of Neanderthal cognitive and cultural complexity.

articles.title: Urban scaling laws arise from within-city inequalities
methods: stepwise tail pruning with power-law scaling re-estimation
randomization tests for heavy-tail sampling bias
data_sources: Statistics Sweden (SCB) administrative and tax records
articles.core_conclusion: Urban scaling laws — the superlinear relationships between city size and socio-economic output — are driven not by a homogeneous shift of the whole city population but by the growth of within-city tails (top 10% of individuals or firms by interconnectivity, productivity, or innovation). Tail differences account for 36-80% of previously reported scaling effects and 56-87% of variance in scaling exponents across complexity categories. A mechanism of city size-dependent cumulative advantage explains how larger cities concentrate opportunity disproportionately among the skilled and specialized, producing heavier tails — meaning agglomeration benefits accrue to urban elites while the majority of city dwellers are partially excluded.

articles.title: Organic reaction mechanism classification using machine learning
methods: benchmarking vs SVM, random forest, similarity search
data_sources: Experimental kinetic data from 7 published catalytic reactions (literature)
articles.core_conclusion: A deep neural network trained entirely on 5 million ODE-simulated kinetic profiles achieves 92.6% categorical accuracy (99.96% with confidence-threshold grouping) in classifying 20 classes of organic catalytic reaction mechanism from temporal concentration data. The model handles non-steady-state kinetics, noisy data, and sparse time points — surpassing all classical kinetic analysis methods — and has been released freely to the community.

articles.title: The cost of anti-Asian racism during the COVID-19 pandemic
methods: difference-in-differences panel regression with restaurant-level and week fixed effects
ZIP-code-level heterogeneity analysis with LOESS smoothing
two-sided Z-test for difference in proportions
data_sources: SafeGraph Weekly Patterns and Core Places
New York Times COVID-19 county case tracker
articles.core_conclusion: Asian restaurants in the United States experienced an 18.4% decrease in foot traffic relative to comparable non-Asian restaurants following the COVID-19 pandemic onset, representing an estimated US$7.42 billion in lost revenue in 2020. Non-Chinese Asian restaurants lost more traffic (25.0%) than Chinese restaurants (10.9%), driven by collective blame, out-group homogeneity bias, and ethnic misidentification rather than food-safety concerns, with avoidance significantly greater in ZIP codes with higher Trump vote share.

articles.title: Using machine learning to assess the livelihood impact of electricity access
data_sources: Uganda Energy Sector GIS Grid Lines
articles.core_conclusion: Combining satellite imagery, computer vision, and ML-based causal inference generates reliable village-level livelihood measurements in data-sparse environments. Applied to Uganda's electrical grid expansion (2011-2012), the framework estimates that grid access improves rural asset wealth by up to 0.15 standard deviations, more than doubling the village-level wealth growth rate relative to unelectrified areas. The low-cost, generalizable approach enables rigorous policy evaluation where traditional survey data are absent.

articles.title: Uneven burden of urban flooding
methods: sociodemographic overlay and environmental justice analysis
comparison with official flood maps (FEMA NFIP)
data_sources: Wing et al. 2018 (Environ. Res. Lett.) national flood benchmark
Wing et al. 2022 (Nat. Clim. Chang.) national flood benchmark
articles.core_conclusion: High-resolution flood inundation modelling at street and building scale reveals that 425,000 people and US$56 billion of assets in greater Los Angeles face dangerous flooding in a 1-in-100-year event — far exceeding official FEMA estimates — and that flood risk is profoundly inequitable: the more disadvantaged half of the population bears 65% of composite flood hazard, and non-Hispanic Black residents face 79% higher likelihood of dangerous exposure than non-Hispanic white residents.

articles.title: A new urban narrative for sustainable development
articles.core_conclusion: Global governance has not kept pace with urban complexity. Urban stakeholders must coalesce around a new complexity-informed narrative of sustainable urban development that re-centers cities in international decision-making, integrates climate, health, and biodiversity agendas, and deploys place-based localization-first governance to meet the SDGs.

articles.title: Group identities can undermine social tipping after intervention
methods: OLS regression with robust standard errors
articles.core_conclusion: Social tipping — rapid endogenous norm change driven by coordination incentives — is powerful but unreliable. In a pre-registered incentivized experiment (N=805, 68 groups), adding pay-off-irrelevant partisan political image labels to choice buttons destroyed social tipping entirely: identity-linked groups failed to transition to a new norm after intervention and accumulated substantial opportunity costs, while neutral-label groups tipped rapidly. Even a trivial symbolic link between choice and group identity is sufficient to prevent endogenous norm change that would otherwise occur.

articles.title: Sea-level rise from land subsidence in major coastal cities
methods: kriging-style spatial interpolation with variance quadtree
articles.core_conclusion: Standardized Sentinel-1 InSAR processing across the 48 largest coastal cities (20% of global urban population, 2014-2020) reveals local land subsidence rates reaching -16.2 mm/yr, far exceeding IPCC AR6 VLM estimates (-5.2 to 4.9 mm/yr). Fast-subsiding cities are concentrated in Asian megacities. The method enables consistent global comparison of relative sea-level rise vulnerability, showing that local land subsidence is a major, underestimated driver of RSLR that empowers local mitigation action.

articles.title: A citywide experiment testing the impact of geographically targeted, high-pay-off vaccine lotteries
methods: three-way clustering of standard errors (week, zip code, HC3)
data_sources: Philadelphia Department of Public Health (weekly first-dose vaccinations by zip code)
Philly Vax Sweepstakes registration database (Universal Promotions Inc.)
articles.core_conclusion: Giving residents of three randomly selected Philadelphia zip codes 50-100x higher odds of winning up to US$50,000 in a vaccine lottery did not produce a statistically detectable increase in COVID-19 vaccination. The pooled difference-in-differences estimate across all three treatment zip codes was near zero, and equivalence tests bound the net benefit at no more than 9% with a marginal cost exceeding US$4,000 per additional vaccination. Despite strong evidence of treatment awareness, high-pay-off geographically targeted vaccine lotteries are not a reliable or cost-effective public-health incentive tool.

articles.title: Measuring inequality beyond the Gini coefficient may clarify conflicting findings
data_sources: Economic Policy Institute top-income shares (IRS SOI)
US Census / IRS Statistics of Income
articles.core_conclusion: The Gini coefficient systematically loses information by reducing an entire income distribution to a single parameter. Across 3,056 US counties, the two-parameter Ortega model outperforms all 17 competing single-parameter Lorenz-curve models. Its parameters distinguish bottom-concentrated (α) from top-concentrated (γ) inequality, and when correlated with 100 policy outcomes the Ortega parameters reveal 33 significant associations the Gini misses — including opposing effects of α vs. γ on obesity and educational attainment. Multi-parameter, data-driven model selection can resolve longstanding contradictions in the inequality literature.

articles.title: Strategic COVID-19 vaccine distribution can simultaneously elevate social utility and equity
data_sources: New York Times COVID-19 daily death data
articles.core_conclusion: Social utility and equity in COVID-19 vaccine distribution can be simultaneously improved by prioritizing the most disadvantaged communities — overturning the conventional assumption of an inevitable trade-off. A behaviour- and demography-informed epidemic model (BD model) calibrated on 75+ million US residents across nine MSAs shows this win-win holds even under strong vaccine hesitancy. Two novel indices, community risk and societal risk, that jointly encode demographic IFR and mobility patterns outperform purely demographic approaches and underpin a Comprehensive strategy that improves overall mortality reduction and equity across age, income, occupation, and race/ethnicity simultaneously.

articles.title: The Blursday database as a resource to study subjective temporalities during COVID-19
methods: log-linear regression of subjective vs. clock duration
confirmatory factor analysis (for subjective confinement index)
online experiment via Gorilla Experiment Builder
data_sources: Our World in Data Stringency Index
Google COVID-19 Community Mobility Reports (Transit Station Mobility Index)
articles.core_conclusion: Blursday — 2,840 participants across 9 countries, 14 questionnaires and 15 tasks during COVID-19 — shows that subjective felt isolation (not objective stringency or mobility) most powerfully distorts the felt passage of time over days to months. Objective mobility and stringency independently shape retrospective duration estimates. Retrospective duration estimation exhibits an inter-individual central tendency (~15 min indifference interval, Vierordt's law) that is significantly attenuated during lockdown. The FAIR-compliant database is openly available to the research community.

articles.title: Understanding rural–urban transitions in the Global South through peri-urban turbulence
data_sources: IEA/World Bank Tracking SDG 7 Energy Progress Report
Cattaneo et al. PNAS 2021 (global urban-rural catchment mapping)
articles.core_conclusion: Peri-urban zones in the Global South — home to approximately 1 billion people — suffer a predictable service-provision collapse (peri-urban turbulence, PUT) because they lose rural natural infrastructure before urban engineered infrastructure arrives. The PUT framework, grounded in social-ecological systems theory, frames this dip as a hysteresis loop with detectable early-warning signals (flickering, critical slowing down), and calls for high-resolution longitudinal mapping of urbanization frontiers akin to annual deforestation monitoring.

articles.title: Impacts of urban expansion on natural habitats in global drylands
data_sources: Global urban land dataset (He et al. 2019)
WWF Terrestrial Ecoregions of the World
articles.core_conclusion: Urban expansion in global drylands (1992–2016) caused an average 0.8% direct loss of dryland habitat quality, but indirect disturbances within 10 km of expanded urban land were 10–15 times larger. Nearly 60% of threatened dryland species were affected by these indirect impacts alone, revealing a major blind spot in conservation policy that focuses almost exclusively on direct habitat conversion.

articles.title: Acoustic regularities in infant-directed speech and song across cultures
methods: LASSO logistic classification with fieldsite-wise k-fold cross-validation
data_sources: The Music Lab 'Who's Listening?' citizen-science platform
articles.core_conclusion: People worldwide reliably alter their speech and song when vocalizing to infants in consistent acoustic fashions, regardless of language or cultural background. LASSO classifiers identified infant-directedness from acoustic features alone with AUC 91% for speech and 82% for song across 21 diverse societies. In a citizen-science experiment, 51,065 listeners from 187 countries identified infant-directed vocalizations above chance even without shared linguistic relatedness to the vocalizer, confirming that these acoustic signals are universally intelligible. Speech and song show acoustically opposite patterns of infant-directedness (speech: more intense and contrasting; song: more subdued), implying distinct communicative functions rooted in evolutionary bioacoustics.

articles.title: Measuring the knot of non-Hermitian degeneracies and non-commuting braids
data_sources: Cavity optomechanical susceptibility measurements (cryogenic, by request)
articles.core_conclusion: In non-Hermitian coupled-oscillator systems, closed control-parameter loops generically produce braids of eigenfrequencies, not trivial returns. For N > 2 oscillators these braids form a non-Abelian group whose structure is governed by a trefoil-knotted degeneracy locus in the three-sphere of control parameters. Demonstrated experimentally for N=3 via a cavity optomechanical system, establishing that non-commuting spectral braids are physically measurable.

articles.title: Exposures and behavioural responses to wildfire smoke
data_sources: PurpleAir low-cost PM2.5 sensors (indoor and outdoor)
US Census Bureau / American Community Survey
articles.core_conclusion: During major wildfire smoke events, wealthier neighbourhoods search more for health protection, stay home more, and show stronger emotional responses, while lower-income residents exhibit similar awareness but limited protective action. Indoor PM2.5 often remains 3-4x above health guidelines and varies 20x across neighbouring households, indicating that policy reliance on self-protection will have modest and unequal benefits.

articles.title: Event-level prediction of urban crime reveals a signature of enforcement bias in US cities
data_sources: City of Chicago open data portal
articles.core_conclusion: A Granger network of crossed probabilistic finite-state automata predicts individual crime events one week ahead at ~1,000 ft resolution with mean AUC ~90% in Chicago, surpassing deep learning baselines using only historical event data. Perturbation experiments across eight major US cities reveal a consistent enforcement-bias signature: under increased crime, policing resources are reallocated away from socio-economically disadvantaged neighbourhoods toward wealthier ones — a pattern stable over 15 years of Chicago data and replicated in all cities tested.

articles.title: Urban conservation gardening in the decade of restoration
methods: linear regression (occupancy vs. Ellenberg N values)
Mann-Whitney U test (cultivated vs. non-cultivated occupancy change)
spatial overlay analysis (protected areas + urban green spaces)
retail availability survey (n=100 Red List species)
data_sources: German vascular plant occupancy database (ref. 25, ~60-yr timeseries)
German Red List of vascular plants
articles.core_conclusion: Cultivating declining native plant species in urban public and private green spaces (conservation gardening) is an economically viable, participatory approach that complements traditional habitat-protection methods. Using Germany as a case study, cultivated plants show higher occupancy trends over six decades, urban green spaces are spatially complementary to protected areas, and reforming the horticultural market through labelling and cross-sectoral native seed integration can scale this citizen-led conservation instrument to meet global biodiversity targets.

articles.title: Water conservation through plumbing and nudging
data_sources: PUB Singapore monthly water billing records
Administrative population records (2.8 M adult Singaporeans)
articles.core_conclusion: A nationwide plumbing upgrade programme (HIP) in Singapore causally reduced residential water consumption by 3.5% — an effect that persists over a decade and is robust across demographic subgroups. A nationwide peer-comparison nudging programme, however, produced no net reduction: boomerang increases among low-usage households cancelled savings from high-usage households. Efficiency improvements also amplified other conservation policies and buffered demand against extreme heat and air pollution.

articles.title: Machine learning and phone data can improve targeting of humanitarian aid
data_sources: High-resolution population density maps (Meta/CIESIN via HDX)
articles.core_conclusion: Training gradient-boosting models on call detail records from Togo's two mobile operators matched to survey-measured poverty reduced errors of exclusion by 4-21% relative to geographic targeting available to the government. Relative to a comprehensive social registry (which did not exist in Togo), exclusion errors rose by 9-35%. The Novissi programme used these algorithms in 2020 to disburse millions of US dollars in COVID-19 rural relief, demonstrating that real-time phone metadata can complement traditional targeting methods in data-scarce crisis settings.

articles.title: Sustainable strategies to treat urban runoff needed
data_sources: US EPA Clean Watersheds Needs Survey 2012 (EPA-830-R-15005)
Zhu et al. 2021 (San Francisco Bay microplastics)
Prata et al. 2020 (global PPE waste, Environ. Sci. Technol.)
articles.core_conclusion: Urban stormwater runoff carries a complex cocktail of emerging contaminants — microplastics, tyre-wear particles (6PPD-quinone), PAHs, PFAS, and pathogens — that most cities do not monitor or treat before discharge to natural waterways. The authors call for systematic runoff characterisation, more widespread data-driven green infrastructure (bioretention, modular filters, retention ponds), and ML-assisted process control, arguing that green solutions are cost-effective, biodiversity-protective, and increasingly urgent given climate change and rapid urbanisation.

articles.title: The potential of implementing superblocks for multifunctional street use in cities
articles.core_conclusion: Superblock and miniblock potential varies enormously across 18 cities — from a few percent to over 40% of the street network — and is shaped by both street topology and urban density, not grid-like layout alone. The Network Disruption Indicator enables planners to prioritize least-disruptive sites for multifunctional street transformation.

articles.title: A large but transient carbon sink from urbanization and rural depopulation in China
methods: random-forest carbon density mapping (MODIS MCD43A4 + static biomass benchmark)
settlement-distance gradient analysis (Euclidean, using Global Urban Footprint)
data_sources: Tencent location service mobile phone data (2017)
Global Carbon Project fossil CO2 emissions (2000-2019)
articles.core_conclusion: China's mass rural-to-urban migration (-14 million rural residents/yr) generated a large aboveground carbon sink of 0.28 ± 0.05 PgC/yr during 2002-2019 by relieving human pressure on forests near depopulating settlements. Urban greening offset the initial carbon loss from urban expansion, yielding a net urban carbon balance of +0.03 PgC. However, tree-cover growth is saturating and the sink is transient — only deep cuts in fossil-fuel CO2 emissions can achieve China's 2060 carbon-neutrality goal.

articles.title: A mixed-methods, population-based study of a syndemic in Soweto, South Africa
methods: multivariable linear regression with interaction term (OLS, R v3.6.3)
sensitivity analysis (binary caseness, GHQ-28 substitution, quintile decomposition, leverage-point exclusion)
ethnographic scale development (Soweto Stress Scale, Soweto Coping Scale)
purposive qualitative sampling from quantitatively defined sub-groups
geospatial cluster sampling using church geolocations
data_sources: WHOQOL-BREF (26-item WHO Quality of Life instrument)
articles.core_conclusion: In a population-based mixed-methods study (N=783 survey; N=88 qualitative interviews) across six urban neighbourhoods in Soweto, South Africa, multimorbidity and a locally designed emic stress measure interacted synergistically to reduce quality of life beyond their independent effects. Qualitative analysis revealed that financial hardship, family conflict, and fear of medical complications — socially and structurally produced — conditioned illness experiences. Non-medical community-level resources (social cohesion, religious coping, ubuntu) were critical buffers, arguing for a shift of some health interventions from clinic to community.

articles.title: Global urbanicity is associated with brain and behaviour in young people
articles.core_conclusion: Urban upbringing, quantified via UrbanSat — a satellite-derived composite of nine remote-sensing indices (night-time light, NDVI, built-up%, cropland%) validated against GHSL population density — is robustly associated with structural and functional differences in the medial prefrontal cortex (smaller GMV and surface area) and cerebellum (larger GMV) of young people in China and Europe (n=3,867). Childhood exposure (ages 1–15) is the critical developmental window. These brain differences formally mediate enhanced perspective-taking and increased depression symptoms, with effects replicated across two continents.

articles.title: Origins and spread of formal ceremonial complexes in the Olmec and Maya regions revealed by airborne lidar
data_sources: CCGS (Centro del Cambio Global y la Sustentabilidad) lidar
University of Arizona Campus Repository (study site database)
articles.core_conclusion: A lidar survey of 84,516 km² across the Olmec region and western Maya lowlands identified 478 formal ceremonial complexes dating 1,050–400 BC. Reprocessed INEGI lidar of San Lorenzo reveals a rectangular plaza defined by 20 edge platforms, plausibly the spatial template for all later Mesoamerican ceremonial formats. The template was formalized and spread through intensive inter-regional interaction after San Lorenzo's decline, encoding calendrical and cosmological symbolism in built form.

articles.title: Natural infrastructure in sustaining global urban freshwater ecosystem services
data_sources: ISIMIP Phase 2 (5 hydrological models x 3 climate forcings)
Global Flood Hazard Maps (Dottori et al.)
articles.core_conclusion: Across 317 cities and 2,113 watersheds globally, protected wetlands sustain freshwater provision and protected forests improve dam performance for sediment reduction and hydropower, while urban flood mitigation depends primarily on reducing impervious surfaces and expanding intra-urban green spaces. Natural and built infrastructure are complementary, not substitutes, and integrating them is the strategic path to sustainable urban water management.

articles.title: Genomic evidence consistent with antagonistic pleiotropy may help explain the evolutionary maintenance of same-sex sexual behaviour in humans
data_sources: Add Health (National Longitudinal Study of Adolescent to Adult Health)
articles.core_conclusion: Genetic variants associated with same-sex sexual behaviour (SSB) show a significant positive genetic correlation with number of opposite-sex sexual partners in OSB (opposite-sex-only) individuals — consistent with antagonistic pleiotropy. Evolutionary simulations confirm this mating advantage can sustain SSB-associated alleles in the population despite any reproductive fitness cost of SSB itself. Personality traits openness to experience and risk-taking propensity partially mediate this genetic correlation.

articles.title: Banks, alternative institutions and the spatial–temporal ecology of racial inequality in US cities
articles.core_conclusion: Race is strikingly more important than class in determining travel-time access to conventional banks versus predatory alternative financial institutions (AFIs) across 19 major US cities: even affluent, college-educated, minority homeowner neighbourhoods have worse bank access than high-poverty white renter neighbourhoods. The disparity is driven by the concentration of AFIs in minority areas, not the absence of banks.

articles.title: A global analysis of the impact of COVID-19 stay-at-home restrictions on crime
data_sources: Police-recorded daily crime incident counts (27 cities, 23 countries)
Oxford COVID-19 Government Response Tracker (stay-at-home stringency sub-index)
NOAA National Centers for Environmental Information (NCEI)
articles.core_conclusion: COVID-19 stay-at-home restrictions caused a mean 37% drop in urban crime across 27 cities in 23 countries, with property crimes (robbery −46%, theft −47%) falling far more steeply than homicide (−14%). More stringent restrictions on public-space movement predicted larger declines, consistent with routine activity and opportunity theory rather than psychological strain as the dominant mechanism.

articles.title: A quantitative analysis of intensification in the ethnographic record
methods: multiple linear regression with categorical technology factor
energy unit harmonization (MJ equivalents for human, animal, machine labour)
settlement urbanization rate estimation from hierarchical settlement data
population growth regression against Ethnographic Atlas agricultural intensity index
data_sources: Custom ethnographic compilation (520 food production case studies, 40 societies)
Binford hunter-gatherer dataset (territory area / population density)
Sandeford settlement data (early-state urbanization in 6 world regions)
UNL Tractor Test Records (one early-industrial Kansas case, 1920s)
articles.core_conclusion: Across 520 food production case studies in 40 societies from foraging bands to agricultural states, labour productivity increases with intensification — rejecting the standard model's diminishing-returns assumption. A cultural niche construction model derived from settlement scaling theory (A = aN^alpha, alpha approx 0.5, beta approx 0.75) accurately predicts the power-law relationship between energy input and output, explaining over 95% of variance in food energy output. Intensification is an autocatalytic feedback engine, not a response to scarcity.

articles.title: The universal visitation law of human mobility
articles.core_conclusion: The number of visitors to any urban location follows a universal inverse-square law: ρ ∝ (rf)⁻², where r is travel distance and f is visitation frequency. This law holds across diverse cities on multiple continents, visitor-flow clusters obey Zipf's law in area, and the EPR individual model provides a mechanistic derivation — corroborating central place theory and enabling recurrent flow prediction.

articles.title: Carbon analytics for net-zero emissions sustainable cities
methods: mobile-phone mobility data for carbon estimation (cited)
data_sources: Global Protocol for Community-Scale GHG Inventories (GPC)
satellite-based GHG sensors with inverse modelling (Zheng et al. 2020)
nighttime satellite imagery for electricity estimation (Roman et al. 2019)
mobile phone mobility data (Liu et al. 2020)
3D city imaging for construction materials (Mahtta et al. 2019)
wastewater analysis for food/nutrition flows (Choi et al. 2019)
US/China/India urban GHG inventories (Ramaswami et al. 2017; Tong et al. 2021)
articles.core_conclusion: No single GHG accounting approach is universally superior — each of four frameworks (territorial Scope 1; community-wide Scope 1+2+3; consumption-based; total supply-chain) maps to a distinct urban decarbonization purpose. Cities should mainstream community-wide Scope 1+2+3 accounting across seven physical provisioning systems as the most actionable path to net-zero, simultaneously advancing multiple SDGs and reducing embodied carbon in global trade.

articles.title: Olympic legacies and the sustainability agenda
articles.core_conclusion: Staging the Olympics now obligates host cities to deliver both sustainability commitments and tangible urban legacies, two agendas that often compete. The IOC's Olympic Agenda 2020 shifted toward host-city flexibility, yet the real driver of bids remains the host city's regeneration, infrastructure, and prestige goals. Only by working with the grain of the host city–IOC relationship can mega-events and sustainability genuinely converge.

articles.title: Large and persistent effects of green energy defaults in the household and business sectors
data_sources: Swiss electricity supplier A customer records
Swiss electricity supplier B customer records
IRENA renewable electricity CO2 displacement data
articles.core_conclusion: In two large-scale Swiss field studies covering over 200,000 households and 8,000 enterprises, presenting renewable electricity as the default option led approximately 80% of customers to stay with green energy, with the effect persisting stably for at least four years, extending equally to the business sector, and producing no moral licensing.

articles.title: The measurement of partisan sorting for 180 million voters
articles.core_conclusion: Using exact residential addresses of 180+ million registered US voters and a novel spatially weighted distance-inverse exposure index, the study shows that partisan segregation in the United States is pervasive and severe across all place types and densities. A large proportion of voters live with virtually no residential exposure to the opposing party. Partisan isolation is distinct from racial and ethnic segregation and persists even within cities and census tracts, demonstrating that residential sorting by party operates at the finest geographic scales.

articles.title: Integrated vaccination and physical distancing interventions to prevent future COVID-19 waves in Chinese cities
methods: generalized linear model for Rt estimation
data_sources: China national notifiable disease reporting system
Wuhan City Bureau of Statistics Statistical Yearbook 2019
articles.core_conclusion: Vaccination combined with physical distancing can contain COVID-19 resurgences without stay-at-home restrictions, but vaccination alone cannot. For medium-density cities vaccination reduces the required distancing duration by 36%–78%; high-density cities still need moderate distancing even with full vaccination. A mobile geolocation-derived Social Contact Index (SCI) unifies both interventions into a single measurable quantity and outperforms raw mobility as a predictor of Rt.

articles.title: Impacts of transportation network companies on urban mobility
data_sources: FHWA National Performance Management Research Data Set (NPMRDS)
articles.core_conclusion: TNCs (Uber, Lyft) intensified US urban transport challenges: +0.9% road congestion intensity, +4.5% congestion duration, -8.9% public transit ridership, and no significant reduction in vehicle ownership on average. TNCs primarily substitute for public transit rather than private cars, while deadheading miles compound road traffic at low occupancy.

articles.title: Gendered movement ecology and landscape use in Hadza hunter-gatherers
methods: hierarchical Bayesian generalized additive mixed models (GAMMs)
GPS data collection and processing (5-second interval)
data_sources: Custom Hadza GPS corpus (Garmin/BadElf/Canmore devices, Lake Eyasi region, Tanzania)
Hadza step-count model (Wood et al., osf.io/5z8q2)
Althoff et al. 2017 global smartphone step-count dataset (717,527 users, 111 nations, GitHub)
articles.core_conclusion: Using 2,078 person-days of GPS-recorded movement among Hadza hunter-gatherers in Tanzania — the largest such corpus for any traditional society — men walked ~60% further per day, explored 3–4× more landscape, followed more sinuous paths and were far more likely to travel alone than women. These gendered differences match foraging ecology predictions (rarer, mobile prey vs. abundant plants) and suggest the sexual division of labour co-evolved with divergent spatial behaviour and, likely, spatial cognition.

articles.title: Increased air pollution exposure among the Chinese population during the national quarantine in 2020
methods: ensemble deep-learning data fusion (CMAQ + CNEMC observations)
dynamic migration modelling (census + Baidu mobility data)
data_sources: CNEMC National Urban Air Quality Real-time Platform
National household energy survey (Shen et al. 2019) + China Statistical Yearbook
articles.core_conclusion: Despite a 16.6 ug/m3 decrease in ambient (outdoor) PM2.5 during China's COVID-19 national quarantine, total population-weighted PM2.5 exposure increased by 5.7 ug/m3 (95% CI: 1.2-11.0). The increase was driven by urban-to-rural migration frozen by the quarantine, trapping more people in rural households dependent on solid fuels. The quarantine deepened health inequality: rural and lower-income populations bore disproportionate exposure increases while urban populations saw decreases.

articles.title: Counterevidence of crime-reduction effects from federal grants of military equipment to local police
articles.core_conclusion: Prior studies claiming the US federal 1033 Program reduces crime are not credible: the 2014 NPR FOIA county-level data on which they relied are fundamentally flawed (only 2% exact match to agency-level audit data). Using cleaner agency-level LESO inventory data across approximately 9,000 law enforcement agencies (2010-2015), no evidence emerges that surplus military equipment transfers reduce violent or nonviolent crime rates. Discrepant prior findings are attributable to data problems and ecological fallacy from county-level aggregation.

articles.title: The growth equation of cities
data_sources: US Census Bureau American Community Survey 2013-2017
articles.core_conclusion: City population growth is governed by rare, large interurban migratory shocks that follow a Lévy-stable (heavy-tailed) distribution rather than the Gaussian shocks assumed by Gibrat's law. A stochastic differential equation built on this insight reproduces the full city-size distribution across Canada, France, the UK, and the USA, demonstrates that Zipf's law does not hold universally due to finite-time effects, and correctly predicts the observed frequency of large rank changes in city hierarchies.

articles.title: The scales of human mobility
data_sources: GADM Database of Global Administrative Areas v3.6
articles.core_conclusion: The scale-free result in human mobility is an aggregation artifact: individual displacements within spatial containers follow lognormal distributions with characteristic sizes, and the apparent power-law emerges only when movements across different containers are pooled. A hierarchical container model fitted to trajectories of more than 700,000 individuals recovers familiar geographic scales (neighbourhood, city, region, country), generates realistic synthetic traces, and reveals systematic differences in mobility behaviour across countries, gender groups, and urban-rural areas.

articles.title: Mobility network models of COVID-19 explain inequities and inform reopening
articles.core_conclusion: A metapopulation SEIR model driven by fine-grained hourly mobility networks of 98 million people across 10 US metro areas accurately fits real COVID-19 case trajectories and identifies two structural mechanisms behind racial and socioeconomic infection disparities: lower-income groups reduced mobility less and visited more crowded venues. Targeted occupancy caps — not uniform mobility reductions — can cut infections by more than 80% while preserving 58% of visits, but must account for disproportionate burden on disadvantaged neighborhoods.

articles.title: Air pollution as a determinant of food delivery and related plastic waste
methods: linear probability model with individual fixed effects
data_sources: MAP panel survey of office workers (Beijing, Shenyang, Shijiazhuang)
Online food delivery platform order book (Beijing, 2016, proprietary)
Chinese Ministry of Environmental Protection (CMEP) PM2.5 monitoring network
NOAA radiosonde upper-air vertical thermal gradient data
Chinese Meteorological Administration (CMA) surface weather records (hourly)
US State Department embassy PM2.5 readings (cross-validation)
articles.core_conclusion: Severe ambient PM2.5 air pollution is a causal behavioral driver of food delivery consumption in urban China: a 100 µg m⁻³ rise in PM2.5 increased delivery ordering by 43% of the sample mean among office workers (2SLS: +13 pp on 30% base) and by 7.2% in a broad platform user base. Because delivered meals carry ~54 g of disposable plastic versus ~6.6 g for walk-to-restaurant meals, air pollution control brings a substantial plastic waste co-benefit — an estimated 2.6 million fewer delivered meals per day if China's PM2.5 fell by 100 µg m⁻³ nationally.

articles.title: Learning is inhibited by heat exposure, both internationally and within the United States
articles.core_conclusion: Heat exposure during school days causally reduces the rate of learning: each additional hot school day (above 26.7 °C) lowers international PISA scores by 0.22% of a standard deviation and US SEDA scores by 0.07%. The effect is concentrated exclusively on school days, confirming a learning-time mechanism. Impacts are two to three times larger for lower-income and minority students, meaning climate change will widen existing achievement and economic-mobility gaps.

articles.title: Local exposure to inequality raises support of people of low wealth for taxing the wealthy
methods: multilevel OLS with clustered standard errors
Gini coefficient construction from census micro-data
data_sources: Field experiment — Soweto pedestrian intercepts (7 sites)
Observational survey — low-SES respondents, Gauteng province
South African Census 2011 (Statistics South Africa) — SAL-level Gini
articles.core_conclusion: A placebo-controlled field experiment in Soweto shows that incidental exposure to a high-status car raises the probability of low-SES pedestrians signing a wealth-tax petition by eleven percentage points (ITT estimate). A companion observational survey (n=19,884, Gauteng) confirms that higher neighbourhood Gini is positively associated with redistribution support. However, a suppressing placebo effect — consistent with reduced political efficacy from upward social comparison — makes the net macro-political consequence uncertain.

articles.title: Seeing COVID-19 through an urban lens
data_sources: UN Policy Brief COVID-19 in an Urban World (2020)
Larcom, Rauch & Willems Q. J. Econ. (2017)
articles.core_conclusion: Pandemic responses can produce healthier and more sustainable societies only if urban equality is placed at the centre of policy. COVID-19 contagion, economic recovery, and sustainability are inseparable from structural urban inequalities. Cities must be at the multilateral table of pandemic planning, not sidelined by state-centric governance.

articles.title: Identification of the human DPR core promoter element using machine learning
methods: HARPE (high-throughput analysis of randomized promoter elements)
articles.core_conclusion: Using HARPE to generate hundreds of thousands of promoter sequence variants with known transcriptional strengths, and support vector regression (SVR) trained on those variants, the authors identify the downstream core promoter region (DPR) as a functionally important, widely used element in human promoters. SVR substantially outperforms consensus/PWM-based methods by capturing sequence-context interactions. A DPR-TATA duality is revealed: most active human promoters carry one or the other element but rarely both, demonstrating that machine learning can decode functional DNA motifs from comprehensive sequence variant landscapes.

articles.title: Changing occupational structures and residential segregation in New York, London and Tokyo
data_sources: UK Office for National Statistics Census (via Casweb / Nomis / INFUSE)
Japan Statistics Bureau Census (via e-Stat / SINFONICA)
UK New Earnings Survey (NES) and Annual Survey of Hours and Earnings (ASHE)
articles.core_conclusion: All three global cities (New York, London, Tokyo) display consistent workforce professionalization — growing high-income and shrinking low-income occupational shares — from 1980 to 2010-2011, refuting Sassen's social polarization thesis. Despite large occupational shifts, city-wide segregation indices (ID, MDI) changed only modestly, while the underlying social geography changed substantially: gentrification of inner-city areas and suburbanization of poverty are the defining megatrends. Tokyo remains the least segregated city, linked to lower income inequality and stricter immigration control.

articles.title: Modelling the impact of testing, contact tracing and household quarantine on second waves of COVID-19
methods: Monte Carlo simulation (10,000 runs per scenario)
data_sources: Cuebiq GPS mobility (Data for Good programme)
Mistry et al. contact matrices (9-country synthesis)
articles.core_conclusion: In a synthetic agent-based model of ~85,000 agents in the Boston metropolitan area, lifting social-distancing restrictions without supplementary measures triggers a second COVID-19 wave with R_t rising to ~2.05 and ICU demand exceeding capacity. A Lift-with-Enhanced-Testing (LET) strategy combining 50% symptomatic detection, home isolation, and household quarantine of 40% of traced contacts keeps ICU demand within healthcare capacity while quarantining only ~9% of the population at any given time, enabling gradual economic reopening without herd immunity.

articles.title: The short-term impacts of COVID-19 lockdown on urban air pollution in China
data_sources: China National Urban Air Quality Real-Time Publishing Platform (Ministry of Ecology and Environment)
Wikipedia COVID-19 pandemic lockdown in Hubei + news media + government announcements
2018 China City Statistical Yearbook (National Bureau of Statistics of China)
articles.core_conclusion: COVID-19 city lockdowns in China reduced the AQI by 19.84 points (17%) and PM2.5 by 14.07 µg/m3 (17%) in locked-down cities relative to controls; non-locked-down cities also improved by 6.34 AQI points versus the prior year due to national containment measures. Despite this, PM2.5 remained four times above WHO limits throughout, exposing coal-fired heating as a persistent source and making city lockdowns an unsustainable environmental policy instrument compared with targeted regulation.

articles.title: Global supply-chain effects of COVID-19 control measures
articles.core_conclusion: COVID-19 lockdown supply-chain losses depend primarily on the number of countries imposing restrictions and are more sensitive to lockdown duration than to strictness. Global spread could cost up to 40% of global value added. Earlier, stricter, and shorter lockdowns minimize total losses; a gradual go-slow reopening is economically preferable to rapid reopening followed by a second wave. Pandemic control is a global public good requiring collective international action.

articles.title: Mapping global variation in human mobility
data_sources: GPWv4 (Gridded Population of the World v4)
CIA World Data Bank II via R mapdata package
articles.core_conclusion: Aggregated data from over 300 million smartphones across nearly all countries reveal a globally comprehensive human mobility typology. Universal power-law mobility patterns hold worldwide, but their parameters are strongly stratified by sociodemographic development: movement declines 40% more rapidly and power-law behavior kicks in at 10 times shorter distances (mean 5 km vs. 51 km) in low-income versus high-income settings. Cross-border movements are predominantly bidirectional between economically similar countries, with proximity to borders amplifying international travel 83-fold within 20 km.

articles.title: Global spread of local cyclone damages through urban trade networks
data_sources: UNEP/GRID Tropical Cyclones Wind Speed Buffers 1968-2009
articles.core_conclusion: Cyclone damages propagate globally through urban trade networks: secondary economic impacts can reach up to three times direct damages for the most severe storms. Cities on the global semi-periphery — highly trade-dependent but with few supply relationships — face the greatest bottleneck vulnerability. Urban climate adaptation requires restructuring trade network linkages, not only physical infrastructure.

articles.title: High-spatiotemporal-resolution mapping of global urban change from 1985 to 2015
data_sources: Landsat archive (5/7/8) via Google Earth Engine
ESA Climate Change Initiative (ESA-CCI) land cover
Gridded Population of the World v4 (GPW)
articles.core_conclusion: Global urban land expanded by 9,687 km² per year from 1985 to 2015 at 30 m annual resolution — four times faster than prior estimates — and at a rate substantially exceeding population growth (80% vs. 52%), meaning much new development served commercial/industrial rather than housing purposes. Approximately 70% of urban expansion occurred at the expense of agricultural land.

articles.title: Green and grow
articles.core_conclusion: A growing, increasingly affluent and urban human population is intensifying demand for more food produced more sustainably. Sustainable intensification — raising yields on existing farmland with fewer harmful inputs — is both urgent and information-intensive, requiring advances in digital agriculture, energy accounting, and multi-scale environmental monitoring.

articles.title: Complex economic activities concentrate in large cities
methods: robustness checks with alternative city definitions
articles.core_conclusion: The spatial concentration of productive activities increases systematically with their complexity: complex activities (biotechnology, neurobiology, semiconductors) concentrate disproportionately in a few large cities, with complexity explaining 40-80% of the variance in urban scaling exponents across occupations, industries, scientific fields and technologies across 353 US MSAs. Historical patent data from 1850-2010 show that this concentration has increased continuously over 150 years, suggesting a self-reinforcing cycle between complexity growth and urbanization that underpins rising spatial inequality.

articles.title: Research gaps in knowledge of the impact of urban growth on biodiversity
methods: spatial overlay analysis (urban growth forecasts × land cover)
indirect impact scoping (FAO agricultural land footprint)
data_sources: Seto et al. urban growth scenarios (PNAS 2012/2013)
Gridded Population of the World v4 (GPW v4, CIESIN)
Newbold et al. 2015 site-level biodiversity data (Nature)
McDonald et al. 2018 priority ecoregion data (Nature Conservancy)
articles.core_conclusion: A systematic review of 922 studies finds urban growth will convert 290,000 km² of natural habitat to urban land between 2000 and 2030, yet the science is geographically biased toward high-income countries — leaving regions forecast to suffer the greatest biodiversity loss the least studied. Indirect impacts (food consumption, water, pollution teleconnections) likely dwarf direct habitat loss in total area affected, but only 34% of studies have quantified them.

articles.title: How transit scaling shapes cities
methods: Pearson correlation with historical city size
articles.core_conclusion: Transit accessibility to jobs scales sublinearly with transit rider density within each of 48 US MSAs (scaling exponent always below 1), but larger cities have higher scaling coefficients indicating greater returns on transit investment. Between cities the scaling is superlinear (exponent 1.34, R2=0.91). The 45-minute isochrone best predicts transit commuter density. Transit investment should prioritize mature, well-developed urban regions over greenfield sites.

articles.title: Integrated stormwater inflow control for sewers and green structures in urban landscapes
data_sources: Danish Agency for Data Supply and Efficiency Map 6170_710
articles.core_conclusion: Integrated stormwater inflow control — dynamically routing runoff between underground sewers and aboveground cloudburst roads and green retention spaces via MPC — eliminates CSO for small events and achieves an average 12.4% CSO reduction (98.4% of theoretical maximum) in a Copenhagen proof-of-concept using only 7% of the catchment as controllable area. Synergy across infrastructure types outperforms isolated control and offers a more sustainable, cost-effective path than grey-infrastructure expansion.

articles.title: Magnitude of urban heat islands largely explained by climate and population
data_sources: Global Urban Heat Island Data Set 2013 (SEDAC/CIESIN)
articles.core_conclusion: Urban heat island intensity worldwide is primarily explained by two variables: background climate (mean annual precipitation) and city population size. A coarse-grained biophysical model reveals that urban–rural differences in evapotranspiration and convection efficiency are the dominant warming drivers. Greening and albedo strategies are most effective in dry cities, while tropical cities require fundamentally different cooling solutions.

articles.title: Passive cooling in an urban setting
data_sources: Fisheye photograph (CU Boulder Engineering Center roof)
Zhou et al. (2019) beam-shaping experiment (companion paper)
articles.core_conclusion: Passive radiative cooling is substantially impaired in urban environments because nearby tall buildings reduce the sky-view factor and add re-radiation from warmer building surfaces. A thermal beam-shaping design using transformation optics redirects infrared emission toward the open sky, demonstrating ~10 deg C sub-ambient cooling even with a material of ~10% solar absorptance, making urban passive cooling feasible for the first time.

articles.title: China's CO2 peak before 2030 implied from characteristics and growth of cities
data_sources: World Bank GDP and population projections
articles.core_conclusion: Tracing CO2 emissions from 50 Chinese cities across 2000-2016 reveals a robust environmental Kuznets curve at the city scale, with per capita emissions peaking at roughly US$21,000 per capita GDP (2011 PPP). Monte Carlo projections indicate China's aggregate CO2 will peak at 13-16 GtCO2 yr-1 between 2021 and 2025, approximately 5-10 years ahead of the Paris Agreement pledge of 2030, though diverse city types face sharply different low-carbon challenges depending on economic structure, urban form, and location.

articles.title: Direct and indirect loss of natural area from urban expansion
methods: sensitivity analysis with actual and potential yields
data_sources: Mueller et al. global crop yields
Monfreda et al. harvested area (175 crops)
articles.core_conclusion: Urban expansion between 1992 and 2015 added 38 million hectares of new urban land globally, displacing 24.3 Mha of cropland. Indirect forest losses driven by this cropland displacement (17.8–32.4 Mha) are 5–10 times larger than direct forest losses (3.3 Mha), amplified by a productivity leverage effect because urban areas typically displace highly productive farmland while new cropland is developed in less productive regions. Compact-city policies and farmland protection can substantially reduce these cascading natural area losses.

articles.title: Sustainability and resilience for transformation in the urban century
data_sources: UN ISDR Global Assessment Report on Disaster Risk Reduction (2013)
articles.core_conclusion: Urban sustainability and resilience are not synonymous: sustainability is normative (goal-directed), while resilience is non-normative (a system property that can be desired or undesired). Conflating them in policy (as in the UN New Urban Agenda) creates planning contradictions — particularly the efficiency-vs-redundancy trap. A new trajectory-based framework treats resilience as a manageable tunnel width, enabling either directed (proactive, path-preserving) or abrupt (fundamental trajectory-shifting) urban transformation toward sustainable outcomes.

articles.title: Genetics and the geography of health, behaviour and attainment
methods: Poisson regression for relative risk estimation
linear regression with clustered standard errors (family-level)
principal component analysis for population stratification correction
Google Street View virtual systematic social observation
data_sources: Add Health (National Longitudinal Study of Adolescent to Adult Health, US)
US Census (1990 Decennial Census, 2005-2009 American Community Survey)
Published GWAS summary statistics (BMI, schizophrenia, age-at-first-birth, educational attainment)
Social Science Genetic Association Consortium (SSGAC) polygenic scores
articles.core_conclusion: Polygenic score analysis of >7,000 UK and US young people shows that genetic selection into disadvantaged neighbourhoods does not plausibly explain neighbourhood gradients in obesity or mental health. Modest but consistent genetic selection is found for teen pregnancy and poor educational outcomes, explained largely by children inheriting both genetic and neighbourhood risks from parents. These findings support neighbourhood-level interventions for obesity and mental health while urging multi-level (person-and-place) approaches for educational and reproductive outcomes.

articles.title: Mapping changes in housing in sub-Saharan Africa from 2000 to 2015
data_sources: Weiss et al. 2018 travel time to cities
FAO Global Map of Irrigation Areas (GMIA)
articles.core_conclusion: Improved housing prevalence in sub-Saharan Africa doubled from 11% to 23% between 2000 and 2015, with 134 million people gaining access to better conditions. Despite this progress, 53 million urban Africans (47% of the urban population studied) still lived in unimproved housing in 2015, with inadequate sanitation as the primary deprivation. High-resolution geostatistical maps at 5x5 km resolution across 31 countries provide the first standardized subnational baseline for SDG 11 monitoring.

articles.title: Urban river pollution control in developing countries
data_sources: China Ministry of Housing and Urban-rural Construction
Authors' field survey of 23 stormwater drainage systems
articles.core_conclusion: Urban river pollution in developing countries is driven primarily by deficient sewer systems — incomplete pipeline networks, damaged pipes, and illegal cross-connections of sewage to stormwater drains — rather than insufficient WWTP capacity alone. China's experience with sewer inspection technology, smart real-time controls, and PPP financing offers a replicable roadmap for the Global South.

articles.title: Contributions of sociometabolic research to sustainability science
data_sources: UN IRP Global Material Flows Database
World Bank population and life-expectancy data
articles.core_conclusion: Sociometabolic research provides a thermodynamics-grounded framework for quantifying society-nature interactions across five traditions. Over 115 years, global resource extraction increased 8-12-fold and CO2 emissions 19-fold, with relative but rarely absolute decoupling from GDP. Evidence that social progress saturates above intermediate resource thresholds (~50 t capita-1 concrete, ~100 GJ capita-1 yr-1 energy) suggests deep sustainability transformations are feasible without sacrificing human well-being.

articles.title: Parent–offspring conflict unlikely to explain 'child marriage' in northwestern Tanzania
data_sources: Kisesa Health and Demographic Surveillance System (HDSS, NIMR)
articles.core_conclusion: In a rural Tanzanian community where early marriage is normative, the parent-offspring conflict model — whereby parents coerce daughters into early marriage for economic gain — is not supported. Younger brides receive higher bridewealth but daughters overwhelmingly choose their own partners regardless of age, well-being outcomes show no consistent disadvantage from early marriage, and early marriage is clearly associated with higher reproductive success. Early marriage is better understood as serving the strategic interests of both parents and daughters in this context.

articles.title: Health and climate impacts of future United States land freight modelled with global-to-urban models
data_sources: EPA Integrated Climate and Land Use Change Scenario
articles.core_conclusion: Using a system-of-systems framework, the study projects that US freight truck and rail PM2.5 emissions and mortalities will fall sharply by 2030 due to emission standards, but long-lived CO2 forcing will keep rising if petroleum dominates. Among tested policies, a carbon tax provides the largest climate benefit (24% reduction in long-lived forcing via truck-to-rail shift); eliminating high-emitting trucks yields the largest mortality reduction (36%); and compact urban development offers modest positive health co-benefits (13% reduction in urban delivery mortalities) despite concentrating exposure.

articles.title: Air pollution lowers Chinese urbanites' expressed happiness on social media
data_sources: China Ministry of Environmental Protection (AQI and PM2.5)
Tsinghua CESS emission inventory (0.25 degree grid)
articles.core_conclusion: A one-standard-deviation increase in PM2.5 (or AQI) is causally associated with a 0.043 (or 0.046) standard-deviation decrease in city-day expressed happiness, using 210 million geotagged Weibo posts from 144 Chinese cities in 2014. The effect is amplified on weekends, holidays, under extreme weather, for women, and for residents of both the cleanest and dirtiest cities. Social media sentiment provides a real-time spatiotemporal barometer for environmental quality-of-life governance.

articles.title: Social-ecological and technological factors moderate the value of urban nature
data_sources: peer-reviewed urban ecosystem services literature (>1,200 references)
interdisciplinary review across 10 urban ecosystem services
case studies from Seoul, Barcelona, Bangalore, Toronto, Los Angeles, Berlin
articles.core_conclusion: The value of urban nature is highly context-dependent: social, ecological, and technological factors strongly moderate whether and for whom urban ecosystem services are realized. A synthesis of over 1,200 references across ten ecosystem services shows that efficient, equitable nature-based solutions require matching the right intervention to the right context — and that ignoring co-benefits, disservices, and distributional consequences consistently leads to over- or under-valuation.

articles.title: Urban biodiversity arks
data_sources: IUCN Red List of Threatened Species v.2018-1
articles.core_conclusion: Urban biodiversity is an unplanned species assemblage, yet cities already harbour IUCN-listed endangered species as wild, unmanaged non-native populations. Rather than passively waiting for chance colonizers to fill ecological niches humans create, cities could be deliberately managed as semi-natural assurance colonies for threatened species using ecological models, public-acceptability research, and urban planning — reducing extinction risk while enriching urban wildlife for residents.

articles.title: Urbanization exacerbated the rainfall and flooding caused by hurricane Harvey in Houston
methods: Building Energy Model (BEM) urban canopy parameterization
articles.core_conclusion: Urbanization amplified both the total rainfall and the flood discharge of hurricane Harvey in Houston. WRF ensemble simulations comparing urban-present vs. urban-removed experiments, combined with nonstationary flood-frequency analysis across five watersheds, show that urbanization increased the probability of extreme flood events by approximately 21 times during 25-30 August 2017.

articles.title: Social-media data for urban sustainability
articles.core_conclusion: Social-media data from Twitter, Flickr, and related platforms offer unique advantages — vast scale, near-real-time observation, low cost — for urban-sustainability research across five domains (environmental sustainability, public health, social equity, urban mobility, economic development). Despite crosscutting challenges including demographic bias, sparse geolocation, sentiment ambiguity, and ethical concerns, solutions to most already exist; purposeful pairing of SMD with traditional data and remote-sensing datasets can underpin a new generation of global urban science aligned with the 2030 SDG agenda.

articles.title: Communicability geometry captures traffic flows in cities
methods: communicability shortest path (Dijkstra on communicability-weighted graph)
coarse-grained Burgers-equation adaptation to graph Laplacian
node centrality analysis (betweenness, degree, subgraph, eigenvector, closeness)
articles.core_conclusion: Vehicular traffic in cities flows preferentially through communicability shortest paths — derived from a diffusion-like all-routes model rooted in Burgers' equation — rather than through topological shortest paths. In four cities (Anaheim, Winnipeg, Barcelona, Isfahan), communicability paths carry up to 38.7% more average traffic than shortest paths. This is explained by drivers cognitively perceiving shortest paths as too central (high betweenness, degree, and subgraph centrality) and therefore selecting alternative routes in a subdiffusive, all-routes navigation pattern.

articles.title: Recirculation of human-derived nutrients from cities to agriculture across six continents
data_sources: Monfreda et al. (2008) global crop area (5 arcmin raster, year 2000)
FAOSTAT (crop fractions and fertilizer import data, 2000-2010)
UN World Urbanization Prospects 2014 (city populations and 2030 projections)
World Database of Large Urban Areas - Nordpil 2010 (city spatial coordinates)
Gridded Population of the World v4 / GPWv4 (CIESIN/NASA SEDAC, 2016, 0.5 arcmin)
GPWv4 Land and Water Area mask (CIESIN, 2016)
ecoinvent v2.2 (transport and process energy factors)
articles.core_conclusion: For 56 of the world's largest cities across six continents, human-derived nutrients in urban sanitation align with surrounding cropland demand at transport distances spanning two orders of magnitude (6 km in Rome to 329 km in Boston). High local cropland density and compact urban form — most common in Europe, Africa, and Asia — are the strongest predictors of short transport distances. Recovering nutrients as concentrated crystal products (struvite, ammonium sulfate) reduces transport energy below synthetic fertilizer production thresholds even at long distances, and shifting to nutrient-intensive crops can reduce distances by up to 76% locally.

articles.title: Machine learning at the energy and intensity frontiers of particle physics
articles.core_conclusion: Machine-learning techniques including deep CNNs, RNNs, GNNs, and GANs are revolutionizing data interpretation across the energy and intensity frontiers of particle physics, dramatically increasing discovery potential; critical open challenges remain around model interpretability, systematic uncertainty propagation, and data-efficient training.

articles.title: Controlling an organic synthesis robot with machine learning to search for new reactivity
methods: linear discriminant analysis (LDA) for active experiment selection
support vector machine (SVM) for binary reactivity classification
binary fingerprint encoding of reagent combinations
real-time NMR and IR spectroscopic readout
data_sources: in-house robot-generated reaction outcomes (18 reagents, ~969 combinations)
Ahneman et al. C-N cross-coupling dataset (Science 2018)
articles.core_conclusion: A robotic organic synthesis platform guided by a machine learning algorithm (LDA for active experiment selection, SVM for reactivity classification) predicted the reactivity of ~1,000 reagent combinations with >80% accuracy after observing only ~10% of the dataset. Real-time spectroscopic feedback enabled fully autonomous closed-loop discovery, leading to four previously unknown reactions confirmed by a chemist.

articles.title: Neighbourhood density
data_sources: Landscape and Urban Planning (source study DOI 10.1016/j.landurbplan.2018.03.005)
articles.core_conclusion: Across six US metropolitan areas, neighbourhood type — shaped primarily by city age and historical demographics — produces wildly disparate urban experiences. Suburban 'loops and lollipops' are racially diverse in newer Sunbelt cities but not in older ones; sprawling fringes are least dense and diverse with the most negative environmental impacts. Denser, historic urban grids are more diverse and have lower occupancy, presenting a ready opportunity to house more residents in well-connected, diverse central locations.

articles.title: The urban south and the predicament of global sustainability
articles.core_conclusion: Urban growth since the 1970s has been overwhelmingly concentrated in the global south — 90% of projected 2.5-billion population increase will occur in African and Asian cities — yet the knowledge system governing urban sustainability is dominated by the global north in authorship (78% of top-cited papers have a northern first author), theory-building, and citation influence. Because global-south urban challenges (informality, infrastructure deficits, governance diversity) are structurally different, northern-derived solutions do not translate, creating a dangerous knowledge-practice gap. Systemic reform of journals, funding, academic incentives, and capacity-building is needed to center global-south scholarship.

articles.title: Evidence for a conserved quantity in human mobility
data_sources: Copenhagen Networks Study (CNS) smartphone GPS + WiFi
articles.core_conclusion: Across ~40,000 individuals from 4 datasets spanning 9-24 months, the number of familiar locations each person regularly visits is a conserved quantity of approximately 25, stable over time even as individual activity sets continuously evolve. This location capacity is conserved at the individual level (for 85-99% of individuals across datasets), cannot be explained by time constraints alone, and correlates positively with social network size — linking it to the cognitive Dunbar number.

articles.title: Addressing the minimum fleet problem in on-demand urban mobility
methods: minimum path cover on directed acyclic graph
data_sources: NYC Taxi and Limousine Commission Factbook 2014
articles.core_conclusion: By recasting taxi dispatching as a minimum path cover problem on a directed acyclic vehicle-shareability network, this paper proves that NYC's ~7,748 taxi fleet can be reduced by 40% (offline optimal) or 30% (real-time near-optimal) to ~4,627 vehicles, serving all 150 million annual trips with zero passenger delay and no ride sharing, through centralized dispatching alone.

articles.title: Addressing food security in African cities
articles.core_conclusion: Urban food insecurity in sub-Saharan Africa affects over 50% of urban populations in least-developed countries, exceeding rural rates. Informal food traders — not supermarkets — are the primary affordable food source for poor households in African cities. Current urban planning visions systematically displace informal markets, eroding the food security and livelihoods of the urban poor. Effective policy must plan for the entire food system, including the symbiosis between formal and informal retail.

articles.title: Not a drop to spare
data_sources: City of Cape Town official communications
articles.core_conclusion: Cape Town's 2018 Day Zero crisis arose from the convergence of unprecedented drought, reservoir management failures, and slow institutional response — not precipitation alone. Every major world city must prepare comparable emergency plans, because technological solutions, institutional organization, and social behaviour must work in concert. Water crises will recur and intensify globally as climate change and urbanization tighten the demand-supply gap.

articles.title: Future urban research
articles.core_conclusion: The next generation of urban sustainability science must move beyond single cities and single sectors to study sustainable urban systems, recognizing the transboundary, multiscale, and supra-aggregate nature of cities. Achieving this requires new data, methods, and conceptual frameworks, plus co-produced knowledge with practitioners at neighbourhood, regional, and cross-city governance levels, all situated within the UN 2030 Sustainable Development Agenda.

articles.title: Building a global urban science
articles.core_conclusion: Urban research is fragmented, geographically biased toward the Global North, and disciplinarily siloed — failing to match the scale and complexity of 21st-century urbanization. Building a global urban science requires reorganizing urban data systems, education, research institutions, and science-to-practice interfaces to achieve genuinely cross-scale, cross-topic, cross-location integration, with equity and reproducibility at its core.

articles.title: Urban agriculture growth in US cities
articles.core_conclusion: Urban agriculture in the United States has been growing since the 1980s thanks to increased access to post-industrial vacant land. Case studies across multiple US cities demonstrate that urban farms and community gardens deliver simultaneous social (cohesion, food access, cross-cultural ties), ecological (biodiversity, heat mitigation, drainage), and economic (jobs, local markets) benefits. Sustainability requires coordinated local policy, land-use reform, and spatial targeting to place gardens where health and food deficits are greatest.

articles.title: Water competition between cities and agriculture driven by climate change and urban growth
data_sources: Global Map of Irrigation Areas (GMIA) v.5
World Electric Power Plants Data Set
articles.core_conclusion: By 2050, more than 27% of the world's 482 largest cities — home to 233 million people — will face surface-water deficits driven by climate change and urban growth. An additional 19% of cities dependent on inter-basin water transfers face acute agricultural conflict. A 10% improvement in irrigation efficiency could relieve water deficits for 78% of vulnerable cities, making agricultural water-use efficiency a globally viable urban adaptation strategy.

articles.title: Regional ambient temperature is associated with human personality
data_sources: Mini-Markers Scale (40-item Big Five survey, Study 1 China)
Big-Five Inventory (44-item BFI survey, Study 2 US)
US ZIP-code meteorological data (mean temperature, humidity, wind speed; source not specified in article)
City/ZIP-code socioeconomic statistics (GDP per capita, population density, employment sectors, farming areas, influenza incidence)
articles.core_conclusion: Individuals who grew up in regions with ambient temperatures closer to the psychophysiological comfort optimum of 22 °C scored consistently higher on all five Big Five personality dimensions (Alpha: agreeableness, conscientiousness, emotional stability; Beta: extraversion, openness to experience) in both China (N=5,587, 59 cities) and the United States (N=1,660,638, 12,499 ZIP codes), after controlling for selective migration, demographics, subsistence style, pathogen prevalence, and other meteorological variables. These findings imply that ongoing climate change may produce measurable shifts in population-level personality profiles.

articles.title: Subsistence styles shape human social learning strategies
articles.core_conclusion: Social learning propensities are not species-universal but are strongly shaped by everyday subsistence ecology. Within a single-ethnicity group (Nyangatom, Ethiopia), interdependent pastoralists and urban dwellers rely far more on social information than independent horticulturalists. Urbanization can therefore rapidly reshape reliance on social learning and may alter collective adaptive capacity.

articles.title: Economic insecurity and the rise in gun violence at US schools
data_sources: Consensus shooting dataset (harmonized from Shultz, SAVD, Brady Campaign, Slate, Virginia Tech Report, Wikipedia)
FRED St. Louis Federal Reserve (national and regional unemployment)
BLS Local Area Unemployment Statistics (city-level unemployment)
articles.core_conclusion: A harmonized consensus dataset of 381 US school shooting events (1990-2013) reveals two peaks of elevated gun violence. The 2007-2013 surge is disproportionately concentrated in post-secondary schools and significantly correlated with unemployment across national, regional, and city scales, as well as with multiple broader economic distress indicators. This strongly supports the hypothesis that breakdown in the school-to-work transition — not random or purely cultural factors — is a structural driver of US school gun violence.

articles.title: Explaining the prevalence, scaling and variance of urban phenomena
data_sources: US Bureau of Economic Analysis (MSA data)
US Bureau of Labor Statistics (MSA data)
Springer Nature Supplementary Data ZIP (43 phenomena)
articles.core_conclusion: A unified theory derived from economic complexity and cultural evolution explains why urban phenomena scale superlinearly with city population. Phenomena requiring more complementary factors are simultaneously less prevalent, scale more superlinearly, and exhibit greater cross-city variance. Factor diversity grows logarithmically with population size, enabling prediction of a phenomenon's prevalence across cities from a single data point.
